card.title: Schrödinger's Cat, Actually Explained
card.teaser: Schrödinger didn't believe the cat was both alive and dead. He invented it to mock the physicists who did.
card.coverImagePrompt: A translucent, ghost-like cat silhouette inside a steel box overlaid with glowing quantum wave patterns, half in light, half in shadow, surreal and moody

lessons.title: A joke that became a meme
lessons.body: In 1935, Erwin Schrödinger was grumpy. Colleagues were telling him that particles could exist in multiple states at once until someone looked at them. He responded with a sarcastic thought experiment: fine, then a cat could be both alive and dead at the same time. He meant it as a reductio ad absurdum. The universe has since refused to take the hint.

visual.imagePrompt: A black-and-white 1930s portrait of Erwin Schrödinger looking thoroughly unamused, smoke from a pipe curling around his face

lessons.title: The setup in plain English
lessons.body: Put a cat in a sealed steel box with a vial of poison. The poison is triggered by the decay of a single radioactive atom. Quantum mechanics says the atom is in a superposition — both decayed and not decayed — until observed. Schrödinger's point: if you take that literally, the cat inside must also be both alive and dead until someone opens the box. Absurd, right?

keyTerms.definition: A quantum state that's a combination of multiple classical outcomes at once
Any interaction that forces a quantum system into a definite outcome

visual.imagePrompt: Cross-section diagram of a sealed metal box containing a cat, a radioactive source, a Geiger counter, and a poison vial, 1930s schematic style

lessons.title: Why the thought experiment is actually good
lessons.body: The point wasn't to declare that cats ARE both alive and dead. The point was to expose an awkward question: where does quantum weirdness stop and everyday reality start? An atom can be in superposition. A molecule can. A virus probably can. A cat? Nobody really thinks so. But nothing in the math tells us where to draw the line.

visual.imagePrompt: A blurred line across a diagram, atoms on one side, a cat on the other, question marks hovering above the middle

lessons.title: What physics actually thinks today
lessons.body: Modern physicists have several answers. The 'Copenhagen' view says measurement collapses superpositions — it just works, stop asking. The 'many-worlds' view says both outcomes happen, each in a different universe that branches off. 'Decoherence' adds a realistic detail: large objects like cats interact with their environment so fast that their superpositions vanish in trillionths of a second. So the cat picks one outcome before you ever open the box.
lessons.funFact: The current record for a macroscopic quantum superposition is a tuning-fork-sized object vibrating in two states at once — far from a cat, but a long way from a single atom.

visual.imagePrompt: A diagram showing a single timeline branching into many parallel worlds, each with a slightly different cat

lessons.title: The joke's on us
lessons.body: Schrödinger's cat endures because it captures the real scandal of quantum mechanics: we have equations that work beautifully and yet nobody agrees on what they mean. The thought experiment isn't a science fact. It's a very polite, century-long argument in disguise.

quiz.question: Why did Schrödinger invent the cat thought experiment?
options: To prove that superposition applies to large objects
As a sarcastic critique of quantum interpretation
As a classroom exercise for students
quiz.explanation: He intended it as a reductio ad absurdum — if you take quantum interpretations literally, you get absurd consequences.

quiz.question: What is a quantum superposition?
options: A state that is a combination of multiple classical outcomes
quiz.explanation: Superposition means the system's state is a mathematical combination of several possible classical outcomes at once.

quiz.question: What does decoherence say about why we don't see cats in superposition?
options: Interactions with the environment destroy superpositions almost instantly for large objects
The math doesn't apply to biology
quiz.explanation: Decoherence explains that large, warm objects couple to their environment so fast that quantum superpositions are effectively wiped out in tiny fractions of a second.

sources.title: The Present Situation in Quantum Mechanics
Something Deeply Hidden: Quantum Worlds and the Emergence of Spacetime
